FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data explicitly: 'EARS (Effortless Assessment Research System) app captures three weeks of passive sensing data on smartphone use in a subsample of adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study'

FT_IC2.evidence: Specific smartphone exposure measured via EARS passive sensing: 'smartphone use', 'app categories' (social, games, entertainment, communication, etc.); specifically measures time on Instagram, TikTok, Snapchat and other platform categories

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical test linking smartphone variable to non-SM outcome. Paper is descriptive analysis only: characterizes app usage during school hours with adjusted means (emmeans package) but no regression/correlation with external outcome variables

FT_IC4.evidence: Supplementary material indicates eMethods/eReferences present but main PDF shows descriptive statistics (means with 95% CI) for app usage but no coefficient, OR, or p-values for tested associations with outcomes

FT_IC5.evidence: Measurement/descriptive study without external analysis. Paper describes smartphone app usage patterns but reports no empirical analyses linking to behavioral/clinical/non-SM outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (JAMA, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC3 fails (no association test); this is a measurement/descriptive study, not an analysis with SM as outcome
notes: Measurement paper with no external outcome analysis; fails FT_IC3 and FT_IC5. Descriptive characterization of EARS-measured smartphone app use during school hours only.

FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective ABCD Study Years 2 and 4: 'This study analyzed prospective data from 5356 US adolescents in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2 (2018–2020, ages 11–12) and Year 4 (2020–2022, ages 13–14)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as generic 'total recreational screen time' composite (watching TV/videos, playing video games, texting, social media, video chat) without phone/SM-specific breakdown reported separately. Fails FT-IC2 as no phone/SM-specific coefficient extractable; only aggregate composite

FT_IC3.evidence: Poisson regression and linear regression: 'high screen time (>8h/day) was associated with higher risk of overweight/obesity (risk ratio [RR], 1.09; 95% CI, 1.02–1.16; p=0.013)' and 'low step count (1000–6000 steps/day) was associated with higher BMI percentile (coefficient [B], 3.27; 95% CI, 1.54–4.99; p<0.001)' linking exposures to BMI outcome

FT_IC4.evidence: RR 1.09 (95% CI 1.02–1.16, p=0.013) for high screen time; B 3.27 (95% CI 1.54–4.99, p<0.001) for low step count on BMI percentile; multiple p-values and CIs reported in Table 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis: 'prospective data from 5356 US adolescents...Year 2 (2018–2020)...Year 4 (2020–2022)' with Poisson and linear regression models

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (Obesity, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports screen time → BMI outcome association (not SM as DV); includes individual behavioral/physical outcome measure (step count); no demographic-only predictors
notes: FAILS FT_IC2: Screen time is generic composite without smartphone/SM-specific breakdown. Only aggregate screen time reported, not separable phone/SM coefficient. Study meets IC1, IC3, IC4, IC5, IC6 but fails critical IC2 screening criterion.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study: 'We analyzed 7407 adolescents (51.6% male, age: 12.9±0.6 years) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study (year 3: 2019–2021 to year 5: 2021–2023)'

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposure is video games only, specifically 'video game use' and 'mature-rated video games'. Video games are explicitly listed as NOT_MET per FT-IC2 criteria: 'Console or PC video games' are excluded; 'Video games unspecified platform → NOT_MET (ambiguous)' (rule E17)

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models: 'Greater parental screen time modeling...was associated with higher odds of playing mature-rated video games [adjusted odds ratio (AOR) 1.08, 95% confidence interval (CI) 1.02, 1.14]' and 'Higher mealtime screen use was associated with greater problematic video game use [beta (B) 0.02, 95% CI 0.01, 0.04]'

FT_IC4.evidence: AOR 1.08 (95% CI 1.02, 1.14, p=0.005) for parental screen time modeling; B 0.02 (95% CI 0.01, 0.04, p=0.001) for mealtime screen use on problematic gaming; multiple p-values and CIs in Table 2

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective analysis: 'year 3 (2019–2021)...year 5 (2021–2023)' with mixed-effects ordinal logistic regression and generalized linear models assessing parenting practices predicting video game behaviors

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article (World Journal of Pediatrics, 2026); no evidence of preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Primary predictors are parenting practices (not demographic-only); includes behavioral/lifestyle parenting variables with cross-sectional and prospective design; video games as outcome but not in FT-IC2 scope
notes: FAILS FT_IC2: Video games are exclusion criterion per protocol. Study focuses on video game use (exposure), not smartphone or social media use. Meets IC1, IC3, IC4, IC5, IC6 but fails critical IC2 narrowing criterion (video games unspecified platform = NOT_MET).
